description: The everyday Nigerian stew — deeply fried tomato and pepper base with well-seasoned chicken pieces. The foundation of Nigerian home cooking. Gets better the next day.

ingredients.name: whole chicken or chicken pieces
ingredients.note: Skin-on pieces give the best flavour. Drumsticks, thighs, and wings hold up best during frying. Breast dries out faster — watch it closely.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Fresh tomatoes for the base. Canned is acceptable but fry longer to remove the tinny taste.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: Gives body, sweetness, and the deep red-orange colour. Essential.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: 1 for background warmth, 3 for serious heat. Adjust to preference.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling the chicken, 1 into the blend, 1 sliced for frying.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: You need enough oil to properly shallow-fry the chicken pieces. Do not reduce.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: Deepens colour and concentrates flavour. Fry it before adding the blended pepper.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Nigerian curry powder. Used both in the chicken seasoning and the stew base.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: For seasoning the chicken and flavouring the stew.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Maggi or Knorr. 1 for chicken seasoning, 2 for the stew base.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Add during parboiling. Adds a subtle, herby background note.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: For the chicken marinade. Black pepper substitutes fine.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: For the chicken marinade. Adds aromatic depth. Fresh only — powder is a weak substitute here.

ingredients.name: ginger
ingredients.note: For the chicken marinade alongside the garlic.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the chicken well before parboiling. Season the stew base separately at the end.

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.note: This is the liquid from parboiling the chicken. Do not throw it away — it is your stew liquid.

steps.title: Season the Chicken
steps.instruction: Start here and give it time — the seasoning needs to work its way into the meat before any heat is applied. Clean the chicken pieces under cold running water, drain, and pat every piece completely dry with paper towels. Dry chicken seasons better and fries better. Season generously with curry powder, thyme, white pepper, minced garlic, grated ginger, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and salt. Use your hands to rub the seasoning all over every surface of every piece, getting under the skin where you can — that is where the flavour sits closest to the meat. Leave to marinate for at least 15 minutes at room temperature before parboiling. If you have time, an overnight rest in the fridge makes a noticeable difference.
micro_steps: Wash all chicken pieces under cold running water and drain in a colander
Pat every piece completely dry with paper towels — wet chicken does not season or fry well
Place all the chicken in a large bowl
Add 1 tsp curry powder, 1½ tsp thyme, 1 tsp white pepper, all the minced garlic, and the grated ginger
Crush 1 seasoning cube to powder and add
Add a generous pinch of salt — about ½ tsp to start
Use your hands to rub the seasoning all over every piece, working it into every surface
Lift the skin on drumsticks and thighs and push a little seasoning directly underneath
Every piece should be visibly and evenly coated — no pale, unseasoned patches
Cover the bowl and rest at room temperature for at least 15 minutes
For best results, cover and refrigerate overnight — the flavour is noticeably deeper
prep_notes: Blend the tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 onion together before you start cooking — having it ready avoids any rushing when the base goes on
Mince the garlic and grate the ginger before handling the raw chicken so your hands stay clean
Pat the chicken dry before seasoning — surface moisture prevents the seasoning from sticking properly and ruins the fry later
If marinating overnight, take the chicken out of the fridge 15 minutes before parboiling to take the chill off
steps.sensory_cue: Every piece should be evenly coated with the seasoning mix, looking aromatic and slightly tacky after resting. The smell should be savoury and fragrant — thyme and garlic clearly present — not raw and bland. After a longer marination, the seasoning will have visibly absorbed into the surface.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoned chicken produces a flat, one-dimensional stew regardless of how well the base is cooked. The seasoning must penetrate the meat, not just sit on the surface, which is why resting time matters. Rushing the marinade is a real and noticeable cost in the finished dish.

steps.title: Parboil the Chicken
steps.instruction: Place all the seasoned chicken pieces into a pot, add the roughly chopped onion and bay leaves, and pour in just enough water to barely cover the chicken — you want the stock to be concentrated and rich, not diluted. Cover and cook over medium-high heat until the chicken is about 80% done — the meat should be white with no pink visible when you cut to the bone at the thickest point, but it should still feel firm rather than falling apart. Remove all the chicken pieces with a slotted spoon and set aside. Pour the stock through a sieve into a bowl and reserve every drop — this is your stew liquid.
micro_steps: Place all seasoned chicken pieces into a large pot
Add 1 roughly chopped onion and both bay leaves
Pour in just enough water to barely cover the chicken — about 2cm above the pieces
Cover the pot and bring to a boil over high heat
Once boiling, reduce to medium-high
Cook for 20–25 minutes for bone-in pieces
At 20 minutes, remove the thickest piece — a drumstick or large thigh — and cut to the bone
The meat should be white throughout with no pink remaining, but still feel firm
If any pink remains, cover and cook for another 5 minutes then retest
Remove all chicken pieces with a slotted spoon and set aside on a plate
Pour all the stock through a sieve into a bowl — remove and discard the bay leaves
Reserve the stock — do not pour it away under any circumstances
steps.sensory_cue: The stock should look golden-brown and smell deeply savoury and chickeny — rich enough that you would happily drink it. The chicken pieces should be mostly cooked through, looking white and firm, not pink or raw. The skin will look pale and soft at this stage — that is fine, the frying step will fix it.
steps.failure_risk: Too much water dilutes the stock and produces a weak, flavourless stew base. Barely covering the chicken is the goal — resist the instinct to add more water. Over-boiling at this stage causes the chicken to go soft and fall apart when fried, leaving you with shredded meat instead of intact pieces in the finished stew.

steps.title: Fry the Chicken Pieces
steps.instruction: Heat half the vegetable oil in your widest pan or pot over high heat until it shimmers and a small drop of water sizzles and spits on contact. Fry the parboiled chicken in batches — a single uncrowded layer at a time — for 3–4 minutes per side until the skin is golden-brown and slightly crisp. Do not move the pieces while they are frying; let each side sit undisturbed long enough to develop a proper sear. Remove each batch to a plate as it is done. Keep the frying oil in the pan — do not clean it between batches, and when the chicken is all done, move the frying into the stew pot directly.
micro_steps: Pour half the vegetable oil — about 60ml — into your widest pan
Heat over high heat until the oil shimmers and a drop of water sizzles immediately
Add the first batch of chicken in a single uncrowded layer — do not stack
Let the chicken sit completely undisturbed for 3–4 minutes — do not move it
Lift one piece to check the underside — it should be golden-brown, not pale
Flip each piece and fry the other side for 3–4 minutes
Remove the browned chicken to a plate
Repeat with remaining chicken in batches — add a small splash more oil only if the pan looks completely dry
Once all chicken is fried, transfer the remaining oil and drippings from the frying pan into your stew pot
The stew pot should now contain the flavourful frying oil and chicken drippings
steps.sensory_cue: The chicken should sizzle aggressively and loudly the moment it hits the oil — if it does not sizzle, the oil is not hot enough. The finished pieces should be golden-brown with slightly crisp skin. The kitchen should smell deeply savoury and toasty at this point.
steps.failure_risk: Crowding the pan is the most common frying mistake — too many pieces at once drops the oil temperature and the chicken steams rather than fries, producing pale, soft, flavourless results with no colour. Work in batches no matter how long it takes. Do not move the pieces constantly — let them sit and build a crust.

steps.title: Blend and Fry the Stew Base
steps.instruction: If you have not already blended the peppers, do it now — blend the plum tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 onion until completely smooth. In the stew pot with the chicken drippings and oil from frying, fry the sliced onion over medium-high heat until soft and golden, scraping up any bits from the bottom as you go. Add the tomato paste and fry vigorously for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens from bright orange to deep brick-red. Pour in the blended pepper mixture, increase to high heat, and fry the entire base — stirring every 90 seconds — for 20–25 minutes until very thick, deeply coloured, and the oil has risen clearly to the surface.
micro_steps: Ensure the blended tomato and pepper mix is ready — blend if not already done
Place the stew pot with the chicken drippings and oil over medium-high heat
Add the sliced onion rings and stir immediately, scraping any bits off the bottom
Fry the onion for 4–5 minutes until soft and golden
Add 2 tablespoons of tomato paste and stir vigorously
Fry the paste for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens to a deep brick colour
Pour in all the blended tomato, tatashe, and scotch bonnet mixture
Increase heat to high and stir everything together
Fry the base at high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 20–25 minutes
At the 15-minute mark, the base should be visibly thickening and darkening
The base is ready when it is thick and paste-like, dark brick-red, and the oil has risen clearly to the surface
Any raw or sour tomato smell must be completely gone before proceeding
steps.sensory_cue: The finished base should be thick and almost paste-like, dark brick-red in colour, with oil floating visibly on the surface. The raw tomato smell must be completely gone — replaced by a rich, fried, deeply savoury aroma. If you can still detect any sourness or rawness, it needs more time on the heat.
steps.failure_risk: Insufficient frying time for the base is the single most common Nigerian stew mistake and produces a sour, watery, oily stew with no depth. A properly fried base genuinely takes 20–30 minutes of active high-heat cooking. The oil rising to the surface is your reliable, non-negotiable sign that it is done — not the clock.

steps.title: Season the Base and Add Stock
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the remaining curry powder, thyme, and 2 crushed seasoning cubes, stirring each in thoroughly. Taste the base before adding any salt — it may already carry enough from the chicken marinade and the parboiling stage. Pour in the reserved chicken stock, stir to fully incorporate, and bring to a steady simmer. Taste the combined base and stock carefully — it should be bold, rich, and deeply savoury. This is your one clear opportunity to calibrate the seasoning before the chicken goes back in and dilutes the flavour.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Add 1 tsp curry powder and stir into the base
Add the remaining thyme and stir in
Crush the remaining 2 seasoning cubes to powder and add — stir until dissolved
Taste the base at this point before reaching for salt
Pour in 1½ cups of the reserved chicken stock
Stir everything together thoroughly
Bring to a steady simmer over medium heat
Taste the combined base and stock — it should taste bold, rich, and fully seasoned
Add salt in small increments only if needed, tasting between each addition
The base should taste slightly more intense than you want the finished stew — the chicken will mellow it
steps.sensory_cue: The stew base with the stock incorporated should look like a thick, rich, dark orange-red sauce — glossy and deeply coloured. The smell should be bold, savoury, and layered. Tasting it at this stage should feel like a full, rounded flavour — not flat, not sour, not thin.
steps.failure_risk: Weak or timid seasoning at this stage cannot be rescued once the chicken goes in — the meat will absorb some seasoning but also dilutes the base. Season boldly and confidently here. If the base tastes flat, add more crushed seasoning cube rather than salt alone.

steps.title: Add Chicken and Simmer
steps.instruction: Add all the fried chicken pieces back into the stew base, stirring gently with a folding motion to coat every piece evenly without breaking the meat off the bone. Cover the pot and simmer on low heat for 15–20 minutes, lifting the lid to stir gently every 5 minutes. The chicken will finish cooking through completely, absorb the stew flavour, and the stew will thicken further around it. The oil will settle on top — this is correct and is the hallmark of a properly made Nigerian stew. Do a final salt taste and adjust if needed before serving.
micro_steps: Reduce the heat to low before adding the chicken
Add all the fried chicken pieces to the stew pot
Fold gently to coat every piece in the base — do not stir aggressively
Cover the pot with a lid
Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes, then lift the lid and stir once gently from the bottom
Replace the lid and repeat every 5 minutes for a total of 15–20 minutes
After 15 minutes, cut into the thickest piece of chicken — it should be cooked through with no pink
Check the stew consistency — it should be thick and coat the chicken pieces heavily
If it looks too thick or is catching at the bottom, add a small splash of water and stir
Do a final salt taste — adjust in small pinches if needed
Turn off the heat and rest for 5 minutes before serving — it thickens slightly as it sits
steps.sensory_cue: Every chicken piece should look deeply coated and glossy — a rich, thick layer of stew base clinging to every surface. The stew itself should be thick enough to coat a spoon heavily. A visible layer of oil sitting on the surface of the finished stew is correct and expected — it is a sign of a properly made Nigerian stew, not a problem.
steps.failure_risk: High heat during the final simmer dries out the chicken, causes the stew to split, and toughens the meat. Keep the heat genuinely low from this point — barely a simmer. If the stew becomes too thick before the chicken is fully done, add a splash of plain water rather than more stock, which would over-season it.
